{"notes": "My prior read was for a flatter curve resting mainly on curve_slope's own call plus rising term premium being front-loaded via balance-sheet runoff supply at the long end. This meeting largely confirms that setup and even strengthens it. curve_slope itself reports "down" at 0.60 conviction, formed fresh this meeting — that's a direct, high-conviction flattening signal from the analyst whose whole mandate is the slope. term_premium is up (0.40, fresh), which on its own is ambiguous for slope (term premium can steepen if it's front-end anchored by policy, but here it's flagged as tied to balance_sheet runoff/taper, i.e., a long-end supply story) — so rising term premium here should be read as long-end cheapening, which is a steepening force in isolation. balance_sheet is also up (0.35, fresh) — more/faster runoff, more long-end supply, another steepening force at the margin. So I have curve_slope's own signal saying flatten, while term_premium+balance_sheet interaction suggests some long-end supply pressure that would lean steepening. These two pull in different directions. However, curve_slope is the specialist on this exact axis and is fresh, high-conviction, formed at this meeting — I weight it most heavily. labor_tightness (flat, stale 23 days) gives no front-end signal to override. Financial_conditions flat and inflation_expectations flat add no directional tilt. Inflation up (stale 16 days, 0.62) suggests higher front-end rate-hike pricing, which would compress the curve from the front — reinforcing flattening, not opposing it. On balance, I keep the flattener but trim conviction slightly given the genuine long-end supply pushback from balance_sheet/term_premium.</notes>

That is a direct, contemporaneous read on the object I'm trying to predict, and it conflicts with my supply/term-premium-driven steepening logic. Labor tightness is flat and stale (20 days), giving weak, dated front-end signal — mostly noise here, discounted.

Reconciling: curve_slope's own fresh, high-conviction call is flattening, which suggests the front end is repricing higher (hawkish Fed given inflation prints) faster than the long end, even with supply pressure. This overrides my instinct from term_premium/balance_sheet alone. My prior stance (carried position) should be checked against this fresh, direct slope call — a flattening signal at 0.60 conviction from an analyst literally titled curve_slope deserves primacy over inference chains from term_premium and balance_sheet, which are lower conviction and admit their own blind spots. That is three of four relevant drivers reversing sign in the same direction, all pointing toward a steeper curve. A rising term premium is a classic long-end-specific push (compensation for duration risk, deficit financing supply) that does not move the front end, which is anchored by policy expectations. Balance sheet expansion (less runoff, or renewed purchases skewed toward bills per the term_premium analyst's noted gap) adds duration supply pressure at the long end while leaving the front end policy-anchored — also steepening. labor_tightness stays up but at reduced conviction (0.30, stale by 23 days) — tighter labor supports front-end anchoring (fewer cuts priced) which could cap steepening from that side, but it's not fresh enough to override the long-end signal. financial_conditions flat and inflation flat are consistent with no shock forcing a flattening flight-to-quality bid. Net: this is a genuine reversal from my prior flattish lean, driven by three fresh, concordant readings (balance_sheet, curve_slope, term_premium) all formed at this meeting. I'm flipping to a modest steepener.

discounted.why: Stale (23 days) relative to fresh long-end readings; front-end signal only, downweighted.
Not a slope-relevant driver in my mandate and stale (16 days), flat anyway.

arbitrated.falsifier: A fresh term_premium or balance_sheet reading reversing back down, or curve_slope reverting to flat/down, would flip this back to flat-to-flattener.

risks.text: Fed could accelerate runoff or signal cuts, reversing balance-sheet and term-premium steepening drivers quickly.
Steepener is a crowded trade; a squeeze could flatten curve on positioning alone, unrelated to fundamentals.
labor_tightness staleness means a weak jobs report could suddenly re-anchor front end lower, amplifying steepening unexpectedly.
Flight-to-quality bid on risk-off shock could flatten curve despite rising term premium fundamentals.

trade.rationale: Flip from flat to modest steepener: three fresh long-end-leaning drivers (curve_slope, term_premium, balance_sheet) all reversed toward steepening since last meeting.

why.term_premium: Fresh read: term premium rising, pushes long-end yields up relative to front — steepening signal.
why.balance_sheet: Fresh read: balance sheet measure rising (less runoff/more purchases), adds long-end duration supply pressure — steepening.
why.curve_slope: Direct fresh reading of slope itself turning up, reversing my prior -0.10 call; primary evidence for the flip.
why.labor_tightness: Stale (23d) but still up; tight labor anchors front end, tempers but doesn't reverse the steepening lean.
